text: Hello, this is a case of da Vinci ovarian cystectomy. This patient was an unmarried girl so we have not used here the uterine manipulator. Left side there is a bipolar and right side there is a seizures. So da Vinci ovarian cystectomy can be performed by robotically and it will be much easier than laparoscopy to perform.

The surgeon uses a left-sided Maryland dissector to puncture a simple ovarian cyst after dissecting the outer ovarian cortex. The cyst is then aspirated, and the collapsed cyst is held with an atraumatic grasper on the right side. The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland on the left and a bipolar on the right to carefully separate the cyst from the ovarian cortex.

Due to the three division and the articulation of the wrist movement It will make it easy to enucleate it compared to the laparoscopy. The rigid movement of the laparoscopy is one of the limitation. Another problem that we don't have motion scaling in the laparoscopy, but in robotic you can do motion scaling and there is absolutely no tremor. You don't need in the simple cases to use the uterine manipulator. So it is very good for the person who is a small girl or unmarried.

The surgeon carefully enucleates the cyst, using a bipolar device in the left hand to control minor oozing by applying coagulation. The dissection proceeds close to the cystic wall to avoid damaging the surrounding ovarian tissue.

The surgeon has completely separated the cyst from the ovary and places it over the anterior cul-de-sac. The posterior cul-de-sac is examined by lifting the uterus, confirming the ovary and fallopian tube are intact. The infundibulopelvic ligament is also lifted and examined.

The surgeon examines the cecum and appendix, noting the normal appearance of the appendix. Using a suction irrigation device through the right port, the surgeon lifts the appendix gently to inspect it from base to tip, then proceeds to suction out fluid and irrigate the area to wash out cystic fluid.
The surgeon uses a claw forceps through the right 8mm port to remove the cyst, completing the da Vinci ovarian cystectomy.
